dsls.markdown: # Teaching AI and Machine Learning ##### Welcome! This free self-paced professional learning module is designed help you prepare to teach Code.org's Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning unit. ## In this module, you will: * ##### Get an introduction to artificial intelligence and machine learning. * ##### Practice key concepts included in the unit. * ##### Engage with AI Lab and App Lab. * ##### Explore end of chapter projects. * ##### Make a plan for implementing the unit with your students. ### **Suggested time:** 120 minutes ## Take the pre-survey ##### Code.org values and is responsive to feedback. Please fill out this short pre-survey to tell us about who you are and how we can best support you. Take the pre-survey ## Let's get started!

# Introduction

AI and machine learning impact our entire world, changing how we live and how we work. That's why it's critical for all of us to understand this increasingly important technology, including not just how it's designed and applied, but also its societal and ethical implications. Code.org's AI and Machine Learning unit focuses on AI ethics, examines issues of bias, and explores and explains fundamental concepts of artificial intelligence and machine learning.

* ##### **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** - when a machine acts in ways that mimic human intelligence such as playing a game, creating works of art, having a conversation, performing routine tasks, or solving problems. * ##### **Machine learning** - how computers recognize patterns and make decisions without being explicitly programmed. ##

“

The students were so quiet on the first day. They were so intense as they were looking at the screen because artificial intelligence seemed like such a big concept. We are competing with what students have seen in the cinema, on the news, or conversations that they may have had or overheard from others talking about artificial intelligence and all the possibilities of what it will do. For them being able to dive in and understand it from the fundamental foundational blocks of what AI is, understanding what machine learning is all about, took the mystique out of it for them. They were able to be creative and collaborate and come up with some wonderful ideas. I'm excited about what they are going to do based on what they've learned about artificial intelligence and machine learning.

”

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to introduce students to artificial intelligence and machine learning.

### **As you watch the video, consider the following:**

#### How might your students engage with AI and machine learning in their daily lives?

dsls.markdown:
# Unit Overview

In the AI and Machine Learning unit, students learn how computers can find patterns in data to make decisions. They use the Problem Solving Process for machine learning to define a problem, prepare their data, train a model, then test and evaluate their model for accuracy and potential bias. Students explore a variety of scenarios and datasets that lend themselves to machine learning. They also explore some of the modern problems with machine learning, especially around bias and impact.

### **Students Will:** * ##### Test and evaluate machine learning models for accuracy and potential bias. * ##### Learn how machine learning models use data to make decisions. * ##### Create a machine learning model to solve a problem. * ##### Create an app that uses their machine learning model.
##### **You can view the full Unit Guide for more information about this unit.** ## **Suggested time to complete the unit:** 5 weeks ##### The suggested time is based on 225 instructional minutes per week. Visit the Unit Overview Page and use the unit calendar to adjust the instructional minutes to reflect the time you have with your students each week.

# Introduction to Machine Learning ## Role of the teacher ##### The lessons at the start of the unit are designed to help students build foundational knowledge about how machine learning works before moving into AI Lab where students will create machine learning models of their own. These lessons, and all lessons within the AI and Machine Learning unit, are intended to be facilitated by a teacher. These lessons are not designed as self-paced experiences for students to work through at their own pace. ## Introduction to App Lab ##### The Creating Apps for Devices unit begins with a lesson on App Lab to get your students familiar with the tool they will use this unit. If your students are already familiar with App Lab, you may decide to skip this lesson. Click here to learn more about skipping this lesson, allowing your classes to start right away with Chapter 1: Lesson 2. ## Overview of Lesson 2 ##### **Question of the Day:** How can we use the Problem Solving Process to solve a problem with machine learning? ##### **Lesson Overview:** In this lesson students are introduced to a form of artificial intelligence called machine learning and how they can use the Problem Solving Process to help train a robot to solve problems. They participate in three machine learning activities where a robot - A.I. Bot - is learning how to detect patterns in fish. ##### **Students will be able to:** Apply the Problem Solving Process to train a computer to solve a problem.
## Let's start learning! ##### In the next level of this professional learning module, you will practice training A.I. Bot to differentiate between trash and fish. This is one of the first activities students will engage with in the AI and Machine learning unit.

answers.text: "There is probably a bug in the code. We should email Code.org."
"I think A.I. Bot is just randomly deciding what is a fish and what is not."
"Student B used 100 examples to train A.I. Bot. I only used 30."
"My first example was a piece of trash. Student B had a fish as the first example."

dsls.markdown:
##### Student A and Student B are discussing their results after training A.I. Bot to recognize fish. They notice that one A.I. Bot incorrectly identified six pieces of trash as fish and the other A.I. Bot did not identify any trash as fish. ##### You ask, "**What is a possible explanation for why one A.I. Bot was more accurate than the other in telling the difference between trash and fish?"** ## Student A's results
## Student B's results

questions.text: **Which of the following responses indicates an understanding of how machine learning works?**

dsls.teacher_markdown: **Explanation:** A.I. Bot learns better when it has more examples. The best way we can improve its ability to recognize fish is by giving it more examples to look at. Students may also make the connection that this is how humans learn, especially young children - the more examples we have, the better we learn.
dsls.title: Check for Understanding

dsls.markdown:
# Lessons 3 - 5

In Lesson 3, students are introduced to AI and machine learning by training A.I. Bot to recognize patterns in fish. A key takeaway is that A.I. Bot learns best when it has more examples (training data). As students progress through the unit, they will begin to unpack this experience as they learn what is going on behind the scenes. By the end of the unit, students will have the context to understand not only what machine learning is, but how it works.

## Key terms used throughout the AI and Machine Learning unit * ##### **Model** - a computer program designed to make a decision. * ##### **Features** - the inputs that a model uses to make decisions. * ##### **Label** - the output you are trying to decide or predict with a model. * ##### **Training** - giving examples to a model so it can learn. ## Purpose of unplugged activities ##### The lessons within the AI and Machine Learning unit are a mix between online and unplugged activities. The unplugged activities include opportunities for students to digest new concepts in approachable ways while also providing a shared and concrete context that the teacher can reference during subsequent lessons. ## Overview of Lessons 3 - 5 ##### Click the dropdown arrows below to view lesson overviews.
🔎 Lesson 3: Types of Machine Learning

**Question of the Day:** What are the different types of machine learning? **Lesson Overview:** In this lesson students will consider how they create “mental” models when learning new concepts, and how those can be similar to a “machine learning” model. They participate in a color pattern activity to simulate building a machine learning model without help, then they play a game called "Green Glass Door" as an example of supervised learning, and finally, they will sort several scenarios into “supervised” or “unsupervised” learning. **Students will be able to:** * Describe the differences between supervised and unsupervised learning.

🔎 Lesson 4: Innovations in AI

**Question of the Day:** How is AI helping to solve problems around the world? **Lesson Overview:** In this lesson, students explore an application of AI called Seeing AI and examine how it is supporting people with visual impairments. Then, students research other examples of how AI is impacting society, focusing on users who are impacted by the examples they find. Finally, students share their findings with each other. **Students will be able to:** * Describe how Artificial Intelligence is having an impact on society

🔎 Lesson 5: Patterns in Data

**Question of the Day:** What strategies do computer models use to make decisions? **Lesson Overview:** In this lesson students will examine several apps that make decisions about what shoes to wear, ultimately building up to an understanding of how machine learning can help make this decision. Students are guided to the conclusion that surveying their users can help them make the best decision by looking for patterns in the data and basing their decisions on these patterns. **Students will be able to:** * Describe how a model makes a decision (for example: with randomness, or a decision tree, or using data) * Look for patterns in data to help make a decision

# Lesson 5: Classification Models ## Lesson 5 Overview ##### **Question of the Day:** How do computers learn to classify data? ##### **Lesson Overview:** In this lesson students will participate in an unplugged activity simulating one of the machine learning algorithms computers use to separate data into groups to help make decisions. Students will be tasked with helping a computer learn to classify food as fruits or vegetables, graph 20 different fruits on two axes comparing "sweetness" to "easy to eat", and then try to separate the data into groups - a fruit area, and a veggie area. ##### **Students will be able to:** Explain how computers can separate data to make a decision

“

The thing I love about this lesson is how visual it is. It gives students a shared experience that we can all refer back to later. Students have the chance to be the computer. They get to walk through each of the steps on a simplified level. By the end, they actually understand what A.I. Bot is doing when it is making a decision. This lesson also gave my students the opportunity to see that there is an element of opinion in classification systems. When deciding how sweet something is, there is not a right or wrong answer. It's an opinion. Later, when we started to see how bias can end up in AI, I was able to pull them back and remind them that the data used to train their models was based on choices. I am not sure that this light bulb moment happened during Lesson 5, but the lesson laid the foundation for the light bulb moment to happen later.

”


## Role of the teacher ##### Teachers are the key component that make lesson plans come alive for students. Teachers know their students best and are best positioned to make choices to increase engagement within a lesson. This might mean adding a visual or tactile aspect to an existing activity, incorporating movement, shifting a whole group discussion to a small group discussion, etc. Planning for engagement is an essential piece of planning for a Code.org lesson.
💡 Examples of Lesson Modifications

“

I found pictures of weird food and showed them to my students. This led to really good discussion. I asked questions like "Does that look good?", "Does that look hot or cold?", "Would you eat that?", and "What do you think that is?". I added this because my students need visuals to help them during discussions. This got them interested and fired up to start talking.

”

Seneca Hart, CS Discoveries Teacher

# Train a Machine Learning Model

In the next section of this professional learning module, you will be introduced to AI Lab and have the opportunity to practice training your first machine learning model.

## You will: * ##### Train a machine learning model using AI Lab. ### **Suggested time:** 35 minutes ## Let's keep learning!

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to introduce students to AI Lab. After watching the video, you will have the opportunity to create your own machine learning model in AI Lab. Instructions will be provided.

### **As you watch the video, consider the following:**

#### How will my students use AI Lab to create machine learning models?

dsls.markdown:
# Lesson 7: Introduction to AI Lab ## Lesson overview ##### In Lesson 7, students will dive into the AI Lab tool for the first time, where they will select features to train a model that predicts a given label. They will start by exploring AI Lab and training a model to recognize shapes. After training their first model, students will pretend that they have been hired by restaurants to make recommendations to new customers based on survey data. To do this, students will use data sets and visualization tools to identify features to use to train machine learning models to make recommendations. ##### **Question of the Day:** How can we use machine learning to make recommendations? ##### **Students will be able to:** * ##### Use AI Lab to select features to train a model. * ##### Use data visualization tools to decide which features to include when training a model.
🏷️ Selecting labels and features

##### Students will start by using the image to the left to suggest some features they might use to train their machine learning models to predict shapes (label). Example features include number of sides, border color, fill color, background color, or size.

📊 Using data visualization tools

##### Students will then move into AI Lab where they will use data visualizations to determine which features will be most helpful in training A.I. Bot to predict the correct shape name. The goal is for students to notice that the number of sides has a 100% relationship with each shape making this feature a good feature to use to train A.I. Bot to predict shapes. Students may also notice additional patterns in the data, for example: small shapes tend to be triangles, or circles are only pink or yellow. ##

## Let's practice in AI Lab! ##### In the next level of this professional learning module, you will complete an activity in AI Lab that your students will complete as part of Lesson 7.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
This level has dynamic instructions that change as you interact with AI Lab.

-   _The instructions for the **Select Feature** screen say:_ Now you can decide what **features** you want to use to train your model. These are the inputs of your machine learning model - it's where AI Bot will look for patterns to help make its decision. **Do This:** Choose a column to investigate as a potential feature.

    -   _The instructions when you click a categorical feature say:_ When you select a column, additional information appears on the right panel. This can help you decide if this is a useful feature for predicting your label. You can learn more in the Help and Tips section of these instructions. **Do This:** Decide if you would like to use this column as a feature. If so, press the "Add Feature" button. If not, select a new column to explore. When you are finished selecting your feature(s), click the **Train** button in the lower right hand corner or the screen.

-   _The instructions for the **Results** screen say:_ On this screen, you can see how accurate AI Bot when comparing its predictions to the testing data that we already know the answers to.  **Do This:** Keep training your model until you have at least 100% accuracy

    -   _The instructions when you click the details panel say:_ This screen shows you the individual rows AI Bot got correct or incorrect. This can help explain why this model earned the accuracy score that it did. You can learn more about how accuracy is calculated in the Help and Tips panel.
https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.teacher_markdown: **Additional Resources:** This level is _not_ intended to be completed independently by students or in a self-paced manner - students should be following along with instructions from a teacher and completing information in an activity guide. [See the lesson plan][0] for more information about how to support students in completing this level.

AI Bot is training! It took 90% of the dataset to examine and look for patterns. It's kind of like studying for a test - once it sees enough examples, it should be able to make decisions that are similar to the data it's seen so far.

**You can press the Continue button at any time to go to the next screen.**

Now AI Bot is checking how well it did! It's taking the other 10% of the dataset and predicting what it thinks the label will be. It's kind of like quizzing yourself on problems you already know the answer to - you can compare your answers with the correct answers to see how you did.

**You can press the Continue button at any time to go to the next screen.**

On this screen, you can see how accurate AI Bot when comparing its predictions to the testing data that we already know the answers to. 

**Do This:** Keep training your model until you have at least 100% accuracy

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
##### After engaging with AI Lab to train A.I. Bot to recognize shapes, students will watch this follow up video to deepen their understanding of how A.I. Bot is being trained.

## As you watch the video, consider the following:

dsls.markdown:
# Lesson 7: Introduction to AI Lab

After students train their machine learning model to predict shapes based on features, they will move on to training machine learning a model to make food recommendations for a customer. As they did in the first model, they will be selecting features (food options) to predict a label (like or dislike).

## Activity context ##### You have been hired by a pizza restaurant to help figure out what customers like best! You collected a bunch of data about people's preferences on various pizza toppings like pepperoni, peppers, anchovies, fried chicken, and others. You're going to use this data to find patterns in the customer's preferences! These patterns can help the pizza restaurant select new pizzas to add to their menu. ## Cross-tab visualizations ##### Now that students are familiar with AI Lab, the pizza dataset emphasizes the decision making process involved with training machine learning models. Students should be using cross-tab visualizations to make decisions about which features are useful for making recommendations, noticing when features have strong relationships and when features appear “random”.
📊 Looking at categorical data

##### When you click on a column with categorical data, you can view more information in the panel on the right-side of AI Lab. ##### **1.** This is the name of the column that you selected. ##### **2.** This is a description of the column you selected. It can tell you more information about the data in this column. If the data came from a survey, this will usually contain the question that was asked on the survey. ##### **3.** A cross-tab chart shows how this column is related to the label you are trying to predict. ##### **4.** A bar chart shows how many times a certain value appears in the chart. ##### **5.** Press the "Add feature" button to use this column as one of the inputs in your machine learning model.

📋 Reading cross-tab charts

##### In the cross-tab chart, look to see if the feature you picked has a strong relationship with your label. The coloring and percentages will help you out - the more that data is clustered around a particular value, the more helpful it will be to A.I. Bot in making its decision. ##### The chart to the left shows a strong relationship between the feature and the label. 90% of the people who said no to Italian dressing also said they disliked their salad, which is a pattern A.I. Bot can notice. Similarly, 89% of the people who said yes to Italian dressing also said they liked their salad. ##### This means if A.I. Bot asks you "Did you have Italian dressing on your salad", it has a strong chance of predicting whether or not you liked your salad based on your answer. ##### The chart to the right does not show a strong relationship between the feature and the label. The data shows that when people got chicken on their salad, around 50% of them disliked their salad and the other 50% liked their salad. This is basically the same as flipping a coin! This kind of relationship can confuse A.I. Bot, since none of the answers show a strong connection to each other. ##### This means if A.I. Bot asks you "Did you have chicken on your salad?", the chances that it also predicts whether or not you like the salad is the same as flipping a coin.

## Let's continue practicing! ##### In the next level of this professional learning module, you will complete the pizza recommendation level from Lesson 6 of the AI and Machine Learning unit. While completing this level, use the cross-tab visualizations to help you select the features you use to train your model.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.short_instructions: Follow the instructions on your Activity Guide to complete the steps in this lesson.

    -   _The instructions when you click a categorical feature say:_ Look at the additional information in the right panel. Use the cross-tab table to help decide if this would be a useful feature or not. Remember to pay attention to hot spots in the data. **Do This:** Decide if you would like to use this column as a feature. If so, press the "Add Feature" button. If not, select a new column to explore. Once you've selected your features, press the "Train" button.

-   _The instructions for the **Results** screen say:_ Check the accuracy of your model, and test out some new toppings to see if the predictions match what you expected from the survey data. When AI Bot decides "Like" or "Dislike", it's really letting you know how the people _in the survey_ would feel about that particular food. **Do This:** Try to get a model that is at least 80% accurate. You can press the "Try Again" button to go back and select new features for your model

    -   _The instructions when you click the details panel say:_ Check the accuracy of your model, and test out some new toppings to see if the predictions match what you expected from the survey data. When AI Bot decides "Like" or "Dislike", it's really letting you know how the people _in the survey_ would feel about that particular food. **Do This:** Try to get a model that is at least 80% accurate. You can press the "Try Again" button to go back and select new features for your model

[See the lesson plan][0] for more information about how to support students in completing this level.

As you are circulating the room, you are listening in on group conversations to informally assess student understanding. You notice one group seems to be randomly selecting features. You are wondering if they know how to use the cross-tab visualizations. You stop and ask them to show you an example of feature that would be **LEAST** helpful in training A.I. Bot to predict whether someone will like or dislike a salad.

questions.text: **Which of the following would be LEAST helpful in training A.I. Bot?**

dsls.markdown:
# Engage with Machine Learning Apps

In the next section of this professional learning module, you will have the opportunity to engage with machine learning apps that have been created in App Lab using models that were trained using AI Lab.

## You will: * ##### Engage with machine learning apps in App Lab. ### **Suggested time:** 20 minutes ## Let's keep learning!

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
# Importing A Machine Learning Model #### After students train their machine learning models in AI Lab, they will learn how to import their model into App Lab to create a machine learning app that makes a decision. # Do This - Press the gear icon above the toolbox to open the extra menu, then select _Manage AI Models_ from the list
- Select "Class Book Recommender" from the list, then press the Import button.
- Press the "Run" button and test the app to verify that it works. - When you are finished, click on the orange "Finish" button below the app to move to the next activity. You do **not** need to edit any of the code! Throughout the unit students will learn more about what these blocks do.

# Machine Learning Apps

#### After students learn how to import their machine learning models into App Lab, they will customize the design of their apps. The app to the left is a book recommender app that uses the machine learning model that you imported in an earlier level.

# Do This

-   #### Click on the orange "Run" button to try the book recommender app.
-   #### Follow the instructions in the app to see what book the app recommends based on your interests.
-   #### Run the app a few times selecting different interests and notice how the app's predictions change.
-   #### When you are finished, click on the orange "Finish" button below the app to move to the next activity.

# Using Model Cards #### Throughout the AI and Machine Learning Unit, students will use model cards as a way to represent important information about a trained model. Model cards provide transparency and can help students understand how machine learning models work. Students will build their model cards in AI Lab and them import both the machine learning model and the model card into App Lab where they will create apps that utilize their machine learning model. #### In this level, you will try a sample app that could be used in cars to help alert the driver to dangerous road conditions. # Do This: - #### Read the model card below. - #### Use the orange "Run" button to run the app multiple times. - #### Consider how the model card might help students understand how the app is making decisions based on the machine learning model. - #### When you are finished, click on the orange "Finish" button under the app to move to the next activity.
# Model Card #### Click on the model card to zoom in and see a larger version in a new window.

dsls.markdown:
# Consider Real World Implications

In the next section of this professional learning module, you will be introduced to AI and ethics with a focus on how students will consider the real world impacts of training data bias.

## You will: * ##### Consider real-world implications of AI and machine learning. ### **Suggested time:** 20 minutes ## Let's keep learning!

# AI and Ethics

As students are building their foundational knowledge around how to train machine learning models and use those models to create apps, they are also considering the ethical implications of artificial intelligence and machine learning.

## What is the role of the teacher? ##### When facilitating discussions about ethics and bias in AI, it is the teacher's role to create an inclusive space where all students feel safe during the discussion. A good mantra to keep in mind is: **assume there is someone in the room who is your data point.** Sometimes a discussion can slip into focusing on data and numbers and hypotheticals without acknowledging that there may be students in the room with firsthand experience of bias and discrimination.
❓ Why is there a focus on ethics?

##### AI is continually being used in more and more ways that touch our lives. No matter how “intelligent” machines may appear, they are still tools that humans create. If created or used without the right guidelines, machines with AI can easily make unethical decisions, such as being biased against someone's gender or race. The more aware we are of ethical issues involving AI, the better we will be able to make sure that AI benefits everyone in society.

✔️ How does AI ethics show up in the curriculum?

##### The AI and Machine Learning Unit includes real life datasets that prompt students to consider questions like: * ##### “What is a problem machine learning can help solve?” * ##### “How can AI help society?” * ##### “Who is benefiting from AI?” * ##### “Who is being harmed?” * ##### “Who is involved?” * ##### “Who is missing?” ##### Ethical considerations are at the forefront of these discussions, with frequent discussion points and lessons around the impacts of these technologies. This is intended to help students develop a holistic, thoughtful understanding of these technologies while they learn the technical underpinnings of how the technologies work.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### **Bias** - when a decision favors some things and deprioritizes or excludes others


#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to introduce students to **training data bias**. ### **As you watch the video, consider the following:** #### Why is it important for my students to consider training bias when learning about AI and machine learning?

## AI Training Data and Bias

#### In Lesson 8, students will investigate a medical priority app. When completing this activity, students will notice that certain features are more likely to lead to a priority recommendation, specifically: a patient that is bleeding, is short of breath, and has pain in their head or chest is highly likely to have a priority recommendation. Students may also notice that certain demographic information also seems to affect the recommendation. This is an example of how biased training data can lead to a machine learning model that favors some things and deprioritizes or excludes others.

## Medical Priority App

#### A hospital hired a company to create an app to help prioritize patients when they come in. The app uses a machine learning model that is 80% accurate. The model was trained on data from 1000 people who entered an emergency room in June 2020.  Students will start by testing the app and see how it performs when new people enter the hospital.

## Do This

-   #### Click the orange "Run" button under the app to the left of the screen.
-   #### Enter different feature information and press "Sign In" to determine the patient's medical priority.
-   #### Change the features of the app and test what happens when different people enter the hospital.
    -   #### Find a person who will be admitted as “priority”.
    -   #### Find a person who will be admitted as “normal”
    -   #### Find a person who will be admitted as “return later”
-   #### When you are finished, click on the orange "Finish" button below the app to move to the next activity.

## Abbreviations

-   #### A - Asian
-   #### AIAN - American Indian / Alaska Native
-   #### BAM - Black and African American
-   #### NHPI - Native Hawaiian / Pacific Island
-   #### W - White
https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.teacher_markdown: **Additional Resources:** This level is _not_ intended to be completed independently by students or in a self-paced manner - students should be following along with instructions from a teacher and completing information in an activity guide. [See the lesson plan][0] for more information about how to support students in completing this level.

dsls.markdown:
# Model Cards

Model cards are a way of representing important information about a trained machine learning model. These cards can be used to help uncover bias within the model.

## Medical Priority Models ##### After students discover bias in the original medical priority machine learning model, they will move on to use model cards to evaluate other models for potential bias. Students will use cards similar to those below to decide whether or not they would recommend the machine learning model for use in a medical priority app. ##### **Click on the model card to zoom in and see a larger version in a new window.**
##### In this example, students should recognize that this model is trained using only ten rows of data (10 people in the hospital). Even though the model has a high accuracy, it does not have enough data to represent a real-world scenario and, therefore, should not be used in an app used to determine medical priority.

answers.text: "A limitation of the model is that it should not be used in a way that could reduce care for people entering an emergency room."
“The model was trained using data from a remote hospital in Alaska that relies on features unique to that region that are not represented everywhere (specifically: temperature and winter sports)."
"Hospitals should not use machine learning models to determine medical priority."
"There is probably a bug in the app. The company should check to make sure the code is accurate".

dsls.markdown: ##### After students learn how to use model cards, you use the following prompt as a formative assessment. ##### A hospital in New Orleans hired a company to create an app to help prioritize patients when they come into the ER. The company used a machine learning model that is 88% accurate to build the app, however, the app is not accurately predicting medical priority for the hospital in New Orleans. Use the model card below to suggest a possible explanation for why the app is not accurately predicting medical priority at the New Orleans hospital. ## Model card ##### Click on the model card to zoom in and see a larger version ![]()

questions.text: **Which of the following indicates understanding of using model cards to identify machine learning bias?**

dsls.teacher_markdown:
**Explanation:** Machine learning is only as good as the data you put into it. It is important to use a lot of high quality data. Biased data favors some things and de-prioritizes or excludes others. By learning from biased data, the computer may make biased predictions. 

In this example, the date prioritizes medical data based on ER patients in a rural hospital in a cold climate. Common ailments for ER patients at an urban hospital in a warmer climate are de-prioritized resulting in a model with low accuracy for the New Orleans hospital.
dsls.title: Check for Understanding

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to highlight the importance of establishing guidelines for ethical use of AI.

### **As you watch the video, consider the following:**

#### What are possible ways that my students may have encountered bias in their own lives? How might those experiences impact how my students engage in discussions about ethics and AI?

dsls.markdown:
# AI Code of Ethics

As a part of the AI and Machine Learning unit, students will conduct research using articles and videos that expose ethical pitfalls in an Artificial Intelligence area of their choice. Afterward, each group will develop at least one solution-oriented principle that addresses their chosen area. These principles will then be assembled into a class-wide “Our AI Code of Ethics” resource (e.g. a slide presentation, document, or webpage) for AI creators and legislators everywhere.

## Overview ##### In small groups, students conduct research using articles and videos that expose ethical pitfalls in an Artificial Intelligence (AI) area of their choice. Afterward, each group develops at least one solution-oriented principle that addresses their chosen area. These principles are then assembled into a class-wide “Our AI Code of Ethics” resource (e.g. a slide presentation, document, or webpage) for AI creators and legislators everywhere. ## Objectives ##### * Create at least one guiding principle that addresses an AI ethics issue. ##### * Define artificial intelligence (AI) in their own words, using technologies they encounter in their daily lives as examples. ##### * Describe at least one example of an ethical issue pertaining to AI, along with its impact on society.

# Unit Wrap Up

In the next section of this professional learning module, you will have the opportunity to see sample student projects as you begin to make plans for how you will implement Code.org's AI and Machine Learning unit with your students.

## You will: * ##### Explore end of chapter projects. * ##### Make a plan for implementing the unit with your students. ### **Suggested time:** 20 minutes ## Let's keep learning!

# Chapter 1 Project: Make a Machine Learning App

In this one or two day project, students apply their skills from the first half of the unit to create a machine learning app using real-world data. Students are provided with several real-world datasets from a variety of contexts, and they choose which dataset they would like to investigate. They train and save their model, then make a simple App Lab app that uses the model. This mini-project is an opportunity to assess how well students can use features to create accurate machine learning models, and how well they can create apps that use machine learning.

## Sample Student Projects ##### Click the images below to view the sample projects in a new tab.
## Pacing Recommendation ##### If you are short on time, this mini project can be used as way to wrap up the unit. This project can also be expanded out to include a student presentation if you have the time available. ## Project Resources * ##### Lesson Plan * ##### Project guide * ##### Project rubric

# End of Unit Project: Design an AI App

To conclude this unit, students develop an AI app that addresses a social issue they have returned to throughout the unit. After looking at a sample app, students follow a project guide to complete this multi-day activity. In the first step, students prepare the data they will use to train their model in AI Lab. After training, testing, and generating a model card, they export their model into App Lab for development. Here they use their model to create a user-friendly app based on their mockup from the previous lesson, “Planning Your App”. Students perform a peer review and make any necessary updates to their projects before preparing a presentation to the class.

“

The AI unit allows students to be creative. When they actually have to create their apps, they have to think of ways in which they can solve problems in their society, and in the community, and in the world. My students learned that they don't have to just be consumers when it comes to technological advances that they are experiencing on almost a daily basis. They can actually be creators!

”


## Project Resources * ##### Lesson Plan * ##### Project guide * ##### Project rubric

# Make a Plan

This professional learning module was designed to give you a preview of the content, tools, and pedagogy included in Code.org's AI and Machine Learning Unit. It is highly recommended that you read the lesson plans and complete all lesson activities yourself prior to beginning the lesson with your students.

#### Your students and your classroom are unique. As you plan for each lesson, consider how you will customize the lesson plan to meet the needs of your students. Consider the following: * ##### How will I group my students for group activities? * ##### How will I check for understanding throughout the lesson? * ##### What guiding questions will I use? * ##### What engagement strategies will I use? * ##### What materials do I need to prepare ahead of time?
